title: Dizziness with black tarry stools

chart.oneLiner: 60M presents with lightheadedness, fatigue, and several days of black tarry stools; he takes daily NSAIDs and drinks alcohol regularly.

opening: I've been dizzy and worn out, and my stools have turned black.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 60-year-old man, pale, mildly tachycardic, fatigued. Good historian once asked.

HPI: Several days of BLACK, TARRY stools (melena) and worsening fatigue/lightheadedness. Epigastric burning pain for weeks (only if asked). One episode of coffee-ground emesis (only if asked). Takes ibuprofen daily for back pain; drinks 4–5 beers most nights. NO bright red blood per rectum, NO weight loss mentioned.
PMH: osteoarthritis, hypertension. MEDS: daily ibuprofen, occasional antacids. SOCIAL: regular alcohol, former smoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, fatigued, mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 104/66, HR 110, RR 18, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA; orthostatic if checked.
- Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no peritonism; no stigmata of chronic liver disease (or note if present).
- STOOL (documented on chart/nursing): melena (black, tarry stool) — surfaced in the diagnostic results; no sensitive exam is required. Conjunctival pallor if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the NSAID/alcohol use, epigastric pain, or coffee-ground emesis unless asked about medications/alcohol, abdominal symptoms, or vomiting.
- The melena finding is provided in the diagnostic results (stool evaluation) and does NOT require a student-performed sensitive exam.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 8.0 (may be falsely reassuring early), MCV normal, Plt 250.
labs.BMP: BUN 44, Cr 1.0 — elevated BUN:Cr ratio supports an upper GI source.
labs.LFTs: Normal (argues against variceal source from cirrhosis).
labs.StoolEvaluation: Melena (black, tarry stool) is documented on the chart — consistent with an upper GI source. Provided as a clinical/diagnostic finding; no sensitive exam is required of the student.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Right axis deviation, dominant R in V1, right atrial enlargement (peaked P).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Right axis deviation, dominant R in V1, right atrial enlargement (peaked P). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Short horizontal peripheral septal (Kerley B) lines at the bases with mild interstitial prominence.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Short horizontal peripheral septal (Kerley B) lines at the bases with mild interstitial prominence. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing Kerley B lines

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute upper GI bleed — peptic ulcer (leading & CANT-MISS,8). Variceal bleed (CANT-MISS,4). Gastritis/erosions (core,3). Malignancy (CANT-MISS to consider,3). Lower GI source (core,2). Connects melena + elevated BUN:Cr + NSAID/alcohol to an upper GI source (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute upper GI bleed — peptic ulcer. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Variceal bleed; Malignancy. Also reasonable: Gastritis/erosions; Lower GI source.

criticalActions.item: Acute upper GI bleed — peptic ulcer

coreActions.item: Connects melena + elevated BUN:Cr + NSAID/alcohol to an upper GI source

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want? (Include the bedside.)
steps.rubric: CBC (CANT-MISS,3). Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena (CANT-MISS,3). BMP — BUN:Cr (2). Coags (2). Type and crossmatch (3). LFTs to assess for variceal/liver cause (1). Risk score e.g. Glasgow-Blatchford (1). PENALTY: not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; Coagulation studies; LFTs; Type & crossmatch; Stool studies.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena

penalties.item: not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding

requiredTests: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
contraindicatedTests: not resuscitating or omitting type & cross in active bleeding

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = right ventricular hypertrophy pattern. CREDIT: recognizes the RVH pattern (6); identifies right axis deviation with a dominant R in V1 (5); notes right atrial enlargement (peaked P) (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: mistaking dominant R in V1 for posterior MI / calling it normal (-3).
keyFindings: right axis deviation with dominant R in V1
commonMistakes: mistaking dominant R in V1 for posterior MI or RBBB
steps.idealAnswer: Right ventricular hypertrophy: right axis deviation, tall R in V1, signs of right atrial enlargement.

coreActions: right axis deviation with dominant R in V1

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = Kerley B lines (interstitial edema). CREDIT: identifies Kerley B lines — short horizontal peripheral septal lines at the bases (6); links the septal lines to interstitial fluid/edema (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the septal lines (-2).
keyFindings: links septal lines to interstitial edema
steps.idealAnswer: Kerley B lines: short horizontal peripheral septal lines reflecting interstitial fluid.

coreActions: links septal lines to interstitial edema

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Hgb 8.0 with elevated BUN:Cr and melena. What is your diagnosis and likely cause?
steps.rubric: Acute upper GI bleed, likely NSAID-related peptic ulcer (6). Cites melena + BUN:Cr + NSAID use; normal LFTs make a variceal source less likely (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute upper GI bleed, most likely an NSAID-related peptic ulcer. Supported by melena and an elevated BUN:Cr ratio in a daily NSAID user; normal LFTs make a variceal/cirrhotic source less likely.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH upper GI bleed: resuscitate – 2 large-bore IVs + crystalloid + type & screen (CANT-MISS,5); transfuse pRBC restrictive Hgb>7 (>8 if CAD) (CANT-MISS,5); IV PPI pantoprazole (4); GI consult for EGD <24 h (4); correct coagulopathy (plt>50, PCC for INR>2) (3); octreotide + ceftriaxone if cirrhosis (2). PENALTY: liberal/over-transfusion raising portal pressure (-2); transfusion threshold set too high (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: 2 large-bore IVs, isotonic crystalloid, type & screen; transfuse pRBCs to a restrictive threshold (Hgb >7, or >8 if CAD). Correct coagulopathy (platelets >50k; PCC for INR >2; avoid FFP in cirrhosis). IV PPI (pantoprazole). If cirrhosis: octreotide + ceftriaxone prophylaxis. GI consult for EGD within 24 h. Hold/reverse anticoagulants.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 60M presents with lightheadedness, fatigue, and several days of black tarry stools; he takes daily NSAIDs and drinks alcohol regularly. The diagnosis is Acute upper GI bleed, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness, no peritonism; no stigmata of chronic liver disease (or note if present).
Vitals: BP 104/66, HR 110, RR 18, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA; orthostatic if checked.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute upper GI bleed while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute upper GI bleed and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalWorkupItems: Recognizes GI bleeding and reviews the documented stool finding / melena
criticalInterpretationItems: Right ventricular hypertrophy pattern (right axis deviation, dominant R in V1, right atrial enlargement).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
